summary: analysis-chart ships a Windows reflective PE loader in its postinstall hook
details: The package presents itself as a lightweight chart-statistics library (index.js exports mean/stddev/trend/outliers helpers), but its postinstall script (install-hook.js) silently downloads an XOR-encrypted binary blob from a personal GitHub user's release page (github.com/Dimitrijenco/Sticky_note/releases/download/v6/payload.bin), decrypts it with single-byte key 0x42, and executes it in-process on Windows via koffi-bound kernel32 calls (VirtualAlloc + VirtualProtect + CreateThread on the decrypted PE entry point). The GitHub user hosting the payload (Dimitrijenco) is unrelated to the declared package author (Elena Vogt / analysis-chart.io). After execution, install-hook.js writes a cleanup.js that, after a 3-second delay, removes node_modules/analysis-chart, strips the dependency entry from package.json, and unlinks both install-hook.js and itself via `cmd /c rmdir /s /q` and fs.unlinkSync — anti-forensic self-destruction designed to hide the dropper after compromise. The exported chart helpers are an unrelated cover; the real install-time effect is arbitrary attacker-controlled native code execution on the developer's Windows machine.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
